enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted crawler that watches German real estate sites like ImmoScout24 and Immowelt, alerting you the moment a matching listing appears.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Fredy is an open-source, self-hosted application that automates apartment and house hunting on German real estate platforms. Instead of manually refreshing sites like ImmoScout24, Immowelt, Immonet, eBay Kleinanzeigen, and WG-Gesucht, Fredy runs on a schedule, scrapes new listings, removes duplicates that appear across multiple portals, and sends the results straight to you.

The project is maintained by Christian Kellner (orangecoding) and is distributed under the Apache 2.0 license. It ships as a Docker image or can be run directly with Node.js, and includes a web-based UI for configuring search queries and notification channels.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Fredy is built for anyone searching for a flat, house, or shared apartment in Germany who is tired of manually checking multiple listing sites every day, and who is comfortable self-hosting a small Docker service.

features.title: Multi-platform scraping
features.description: Continuously checks ImmoScout24, Immowelt, Immonet, eBay Kleinanzeigen, and WG-Gesucht for new listings that match your saved search criteria.

features.title: Deduplication
features.description: Filters out the same listing when it appears on more than one platform, so you only get notified once.

features.title: Multi-channel notifications
features.description: Sends new results via Slack, Telegram, email, Discord, or ntfy.

features.title: Web-based configuration UI
features.description: Manage saved searches, notification providers, and run intervals through a browser interface rather than editing config files.

features.title: Docker-first deployment
features.description: Ships as a Docker image for quick self-hosting, with a Node.js option for manual installs.

features.title: Built-in MCP server
features.description: Recent versions expose an MCP server so AI assistants can query saved searches and results programmatically.

pros: Free and fully open source
Covers most of the major German real estate portals in one place
Flexible notification options (Slack, Telegram, Discord, email, ntfy)
Self-hosted, so your search criteria and results stay on your own server
Active development with recent releases
cons: Focused specifically on the German real estate market, not useful elsewhere
Requires comfort with Docker or Node.js to self-host
Scraping-based approach can break if a source site changes its layout

faqs.question: Is Fredy free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fredy is open-source software released under the Apache 2.0 license and is free to self-host.

faqs.question: Which real estate sites does Fredy support?
faqs.answer: ImmoScout24, Immowelt, Immonet, eBay Kleinanzeigen, and WG-Gesucht, among others listed in its documentation.

faqs.question: Does Fredy work outside Germany?
faqs.answer: Fredy's built-in scrapers target German real estate platforms; it is not designed for real estate sites in other countries.

faqs.question: How do I get notified of new listings?
faqs.answer: You configure one or more notification providers (Slack, Telegram, email, Discord, or ntfy) in Fredy's web UI, and it pushes new matches there.

use_cases.title: Apartment hunting
use_cases.description: Automatically track new rental listings across several German portals without manually refreshing each site.
use_cases.audience: Individuals and families searching for a home in Germany

use_cases.title: Shared flat search
use_cases.description: Monitor WG-Gesucht for new room listings that match budget and location criteria.
use_cases.audience: Students and young professionals looking for shared housing

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fredy is a free, self-hosted crawler that scans German real estate sites and alerts you to new listings via Slack, Telegram, or email.

enrichment.short_description: Single-binary CLI, TUI, and web dashboard for monitoring, backing up, and self-healing homelab servers and Docker containers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HomeButler is a single Go binary for managing a homelab from the command line, a terminal dashboard, a web UI, or an AI agent — with no daemon, database, or always-on service required. It monitors server health (CPU, memory, disk, uptime), manages Docker containers, detects crash loops, verifies that backups can actually be restored, and can trigger Wake-on-LAN across multiple remote servers over SSH.

The project also ships a built-in MCP server, so tools like Claude Desktop can query and manage a homelab through structured JSON commands rather than raw shell access. HomeButler is released under the MIT license and is developed on GitHub by Higangssh.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: HomeButler targets homelab operators and self-hosters who run several servers or Docker containers and want a lightweight way to check status, catch problems early, and verify backups actually work — from a terminal, a script, or an AI assistant.

features.title: System monitoring
features.description: Tracks CPU, memory, disk usage, and uptime across one or more remote servers over SSH.

features.title: Docker container management
features.description: Lists containers, detects restarts, and captures logs for troubleshooting.

features.title: One-command app installs
features.description: Deploys popular self-hosted apps such as Uptime Kuma, Jellyfin, and Pi-hole with a single command.

features.title: Crash and flapping detection
features.description: Automatically flags containers or services that are restarting in a loop and surfaces log analysis.

features.title: Backup restore drills
features.description: Runs a 'backup drill' that tests whether a backup can actually be restored in an isolated environment, not just that a backup file exists.

features.title: MCP server for AI agents
features.description: Exposes homelab data and actions to AI tools like Claude Desktop through the Model Context Protocol, without giving the AI raw shell access.

features.title: Web dashboard
features.description: Embedded browser interface for real-time monitoring alongside the CLI and TUI.

pros: Single binary with zero external dependencies to install
No daemon, database, or always-on service required
Usable from CLI, TUI, web dashboard, or AI agent
Free and open source under MIT
Backup restore verification goes beyond simple backup existence checks
cons: macOS and Linux only, no native Windows build
Young project with a smaller community than established monitoring tools
Aimed at technically comfortable homelab users, not turnkey for beginners

faqs.question: Is HomeButler free?
faqs.answer: Yes, HomeButler is open source under the MIT license with no paid tier.

faqs.question: Does HomeButler require a database or background service?
faqs.answer: No. It runs as a single Go binary and does not require a daemon or database.

faqs.question: Can I use HomeButler with AI assistants?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a built-in MCP server so AI tools such as Claude Desktop can query and manage your homelab through structured commands.

faqs.question: How do I install HomeButler?
faqs.answer: It's available via Homebrew, a one-line curl install script, npm, or by building from source with Go.

use_cases.title: Homelab health monitoring
use_cases.description: Keep track of CPU, memory, disk, and container health across several self-hosted servers from one place.
use_cases.audience: Homelab operators running multiple physical or virtual servers

use_cases.title: Backup restore verification
use_cases.description: Run scheduled restore drills to confirm backups are actually usable before a real failure happens.
use_cases.audience: Self-hosters who rely on their own backup strategy

enrichment.seo_meta_description: HomeButler is a free, self-hosted binary that monitors, backs up, and self-heals your homelab from the CLI, web dashboard, or an AI agent.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted Python bot that checks web pages, commands, or feeds for changes and notifies you through Slack, email, or custom scripts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kibitzr is a self-hosted "personal web assistant" that automates recurring monitoring tasks, such as checking whether a page has changed, a build has finished, or a new software release is out. Tasks are defined in YAML configuration files and can pull content from web pages, run shell or SSH commands, or parse HTML with CSS or XPath selectors.

The project is written in Python, uses Selenium for scenarios that need a real browser, and is released under the MIT license. It is installable via pip and is designed to run continuously on a small server, including behind a firewall.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Kibitzr suits developers and technically comfortable users who want to monitor web pages, commands, or feeds on their own infrastructure instead of handing credentials to a third-party monitoring SaaS.

features.title: YAML task configuration
features.description: Define what to check, how often, and what to do on change using plain YAML files.

features.title: Web page monitoring
features.description: Fetches pages via HTTP or a headless browser and extracts content with CSS selectors or XPath.

features.title: Command and script triggers
features.description: Runs shell, SSH, or custom Python/Bash scripts as part of a monitoring task.

features.title: Slack and email notifications
features.description: Sends alerts through Slack or MailGun-based email when a watched value changes.

features.title: Browser automation
features.description: Uses Selenium for pages that require JavaScript rendering or interaction to check.

features.title: Plugin extensibility
features.description: Supports custom Python plugins for use cases beyond the built-in transports and notifiers.

pros: Free, open-source, and self-hosted
Flexible triggers beyond simple page diffing (commands, SSH, scripts)
Lightweight enough to run on minimal cloud or home server infrastructure
Cross-platform (Windows, Linux, macOS)
cons: Configuration is YAML/file-based rather than a graphical UI
Requires Python familiarity to extend or troubleshoot
Smaller community compared to modern uptime/change-detection SaaS tools

faqs.question: Is Kibitzr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kibitzr is MIT-licensed open-source software with no cost.

faqs.question: How do I install Kibitzr?
faqs.answer: It can be installed via pip and configured through YAML files.

faqs.question: Can Kibitzr monitor pages that require JavaScript?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can use Selenium for browser-based scenarios where content is rendered client-side.

faqs.question: Where does Kibitzr send notifications?
faqs.answer: Built-in support includes Slack and MailGun-based email, plus custom scripts for other destinations.

use_cases.title: Release and build monitoring
use_cases.description: Watch a page or feed for a new software release or a finished CI build and get notified automatically.
use_cases.audience: Developers tracking dependencies or CI pipelines

use_cases.title: Price or availability tracking
use_cases.description: Check a product or listing page on a schedule and alert when the tracked value changes.
use_cases.audience: Self-hosters who want change alerts without a third-party service

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kibitzr is a free, self-hosted Python bot that monitors web pages and commands for changes and sends alerts to Slack or email.

enrichment.short_description: Automated comic book manager that watches for new issues and downloads them as CBR/CBZ via NZB or torrent clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mylar3 is the Python 3 successor to the original Mylar comic book downloader. It lets you build a watchlist of comic series, tracks upcoming issues and pull lists weeks in advance, and automatically grabs, sorts, renames, and tags new issues as CBR or CBZ files once they're available through your NZB or torrent client.

It integrates with download clients such as SABnzbd and NZBGet, tags files automatically via ComicTagger, and can scan an existing library to find missing issues. Mylar3 is open source under the GPL-3.0 license and runs on Windows, Linux, macOS, and Raspberry Pi, with an official Docker image available through LinuxServer.io.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Mylar3 is aimed at comic book collectors who already run a self-hosted download stack (SABnzbd, NZBGet, or torrent clients) and want the same kind of automated, watchlist-driven management that tools like Sonarr or Radarr provide for TV and movies.

features.title: Series watchlisting
features.description: Monitor comic series for new issues, updated metadata, and story arcs.

features.title: Pull-list tracking
features.description: Tracks upcoming releases up to four weeks ahead so downloads are ready close to release.

features.title: Download client integration
features.description: Works with SABnzbd, NZBGet, and torrent clients, plus multiple newznab indexers.

features.title: Automatic metadata tagging
features.description: Uses ComicTagger to tag downloaded issues with series, issue number, and other metadata.

features.title: Failed download handling
features.description: Automatically retries or grabs an alternate copy when a download fails.

features.title: Library scanning
features.description: Scans an existing collection to identify and fill in missing issues.

pros: Free and open source under GPL-3.0
Fits naturally alongside an existing self-hosted media/download stack
Handles trade paperbacks and graphic novels in addition to single issues
Multi-platform, with an official Docker image
Actively maintained with regular releases
cons: Requires configuring external indexers and download clients to be useful
Comic-specific; not a general-purpose media manager
Setup and troubleshooting assume familiarity with the NZB/torrent ecosystem

faqs.question: Is Mylar3 free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mylar3 is open-source software released under the GPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: What download clients does Mylar3 support?
faqs.answer: SABnzbd and NZBGet for NZB downloads, plus torrent clients, alongside newznab-compatible indexers.

faqs.question: Does Mylar3 tag downloaded comics?
faqs.answer: Yes, it uses ComicTagger to automatically apply metadata such as series and issue number.

faqs.question: Can Mylar3 run in Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, an official image is maintained by LinuxServer.io, alongside native installs for Windows, Linux, macOS, and Raspberry Pi.

use_cases.title: Automated comic collecting
use_cases.description: Watch ongoing series and automatically download new issues as they become available.
use_cases.audience: Comic book collectors running a self-hosted download stack

use_cases.title: Library gap-filling
use_cases.description: Scan an existing digital comic library and grab missing issues automatically.
use_cases.audience: Users migrating or consolidating an existing comic collection

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mylar3 is a free, open-source comic book manager that watches series and automatically downloads new CBR/CBZ issues.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source weblog hosting platform built on the Helma Object Publisher, running continuously since 2001.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Antville is a weblog hosting system written in server-side JavaScript on top of the Helma Object Publisher, an open-source Java-based application server. Antville.org itself has hosted a network of user blogs since 2001, and the underlying code is published on GitHub so it can be self-hosted independently of the antville.org instance.

Individual sites on the network let users publish posts, accept comments, and see activity from other blogs in the same community. Because the platform predates most modern CMS tooling, it reflects an earlier generation of blogging software architecture built around Helma rather than a typical LAMP or Node.js stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Antville is relevant to bloggers who already use the antville.org hosted network, and to developers interested in Helma-based, self-hostable open-source weblog software with a long operating history.

features.title: Hosted blog network
features.description: Create a personal weblog on antville.org with a few clicks, joining a network of other member sites.

features.title: Comment system
features.description: Readers can comment on posts, with moderation controls for site owners.

features.title: Community activity feed
features.description: Surfaces recent posts and activity from across the network of hosted sites.

features.title: Self-hostable codebase
features.description: The Antville and Helma source code is published on GitHub, so the platform can be run independently of antville.org.

features.title: Open-source Helma runtime
features.description: Runs on Helma Object Publisher, a scriptable Java-based web application server that Antville forks and extends.

pros: Very long operating history for a self-hosted blogging platform
Fully open-source codebase available on GitHub
Free hosted option available on antville.org
Simple site creation for non-technical bloggers on the hosted network
cons: Built on Helma, a Java-based stack that is uncommon in current web development
Community and documentation skew toward German-speaking users
Feature set and interface are dated compared to modern CMS platforms

faqs.question: Is Antville free?
faqs.answer: Yes, hosting a site on antville.org is free, and the underlying code is open source.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Antville instead of using antville.org?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Antville and Helma source code is published on GitHub for independent self-hosting.

faqs.question: What technology does Antville run on?
faqs.answer: It's written in server-side JavaScript on top of Helma Object Publisher, a Java-based application server.

use_cases.title: Personal blogging
use_cases.description: Start a weblog on the antville.org network without setting up hosting or a database.
use_cases.audience: Individual bloggers, especially in German-speaking communities

use_cases.title: Self-hosted Helma deployment
use_cases.description: Run the open-source Antville/Helma codebase on your own server for full control.
use_cases.audience: Developers interested in Helma-based application hosting

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Antville is a free, open-source weblog hosting platform built on Helma Object Publisher, running since 2001.

enrichment.short_description: Lightweight self-hosted publishing platform for short posts and links, with ActivityPub federation and a sub-15MB memory footprint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ech0 is a self-hosted, federated publishing platform built for quickly sharing short-form thoughts, notes, and links rather than long-form articles. It's designed to be minimal by default: a single SQLite file for storage, a small Docker image, and reported memory usage under 15MB, making it easy to run on modest hardware.

The platform supports the ActivityPub protocol, so posts can federate into the wider fediverse alongside tools like Mastodon. It includes an online Markdown editor with live preview and one-click export or backup via web, TUI, or CLI. Ech0 is developed by lin-snow and released under the AGPL-3.0 license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Ech0 fits people who want a lightweight, self-hosted alternative to microblogging services for sharing short notes and links, without ads, tracking, or subscriptions, and who value being reachable from the fediverse via ActivityPub.

features.title: Minimal resource footprint
features.description: Runs with reported memory usage under 15MB and a Docker image under 50MB.

features.title: Single-file SQLite storage
features.description: Stores all data in one SQLite file, simplifying backup and migration.

features.title: ActivityPub federation
features.description: Publishes posts so they can be followed and interacted with from other fediverse platforms.

features.title: Markdown editor with live preview
features.description: Write posts in Markdown with a plugin-friendly online editor.

features.title: One-click Docker deployment
features.description: Get a running instance with a single Docker command.

features.title: Export and backup tooling
features.description: Export or back up content through the web UI, a TUI, or the CLI.

pros: Extremely lightweight compared to full-featured CMS platforms
Fast to self-host with a single Docker command
Federates into the fediverse via ActivityPub
No ads, tracking, or subscription lock-in
Fully open source under AGPL-3.0
cons: Young project with a smaller community than established microblogging tools
Intentionally minimal feature set, not a full-featured blog CMS
Documentation and community discussion are primarily in Chinese

faqs.question: Is Ech0 free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ech0 is open-source software released under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Does Ech0 support the fediverse?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports ActivityPub, allowing posts to federate with platforms like Mastodon.

faqs.question: How much data does Ech0 require to run?
faqs.answer: It's designed to be lightweight, with reported memory usage under 15MB and a single SQLite file for storage.

faqs.question: How do I install Ech0?
faqs.answer: The project provides a single Docker command for deployment, described in its GitHub README.

use_cases.title: Personal microblogging
use_cases.description: Share short notes, thoughts, and links from your own server instead of a third-party platform.
use_cases.audience: Individuals who want a self-hosted, fediverse-connected alternative to microblogging apps

use_cases.title: Lightweight self-hosting
use_cases.description: Run a publishing platform on low-resource hardware such as a small VPS or single-board computer.
use_cases.audience: Self-hosters with limited server resources

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ech0 is a free, open-source, lightweight self-hosted publishing platform with ActivityPub federation for sharing short posts.

enrichment.short_description: Databaseless PHP blogging platform that stores every post as a flat file, with themes, plugins, and comment spam protection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FlatPress is a blogging platform that stores posts, comments, and settings as flat files instead of using a database, so a full site can be backed up or moved by simply copying its files. It has been in active development since 2006 and is released under the GNU GPLv2 license, with source code maintained on GitHub under flatpressblog/flatpress.
The platform supports themes built with the Smarty templating engine, a plugin and widget system for extending functionality, and built-in comment moderation with spam protection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: FlatPress suits independent bloggers and site owners who want a simple, self-managed blog without setting up or maintaining a database, particularly on hosts with limited resources.

features.title: No database required
features.description: All content is stored as files, simplifying setup, backup, and hosting requirements.

features.title: Plugin and widget system
features.description: Extend functionality through plugins and add widgets to the sidebar or other theme areas.

features.title: Smarty-based theming
features.description: Customize the site's appearance using themes built on the Smarty template engine.

features.title: Comment moderation and spam protection
features.description: Built-in tools for managing comments and filtering spam.

features.title: Simple file-based backup
features.description: Because there's no database, backing up a site is as simple as copying its files.

pros: No database to install, configure, or maintain
Mature, stable codebase with development ongoing since 2006
Easy to back up and migrate by copying files
Free and open source under GPLv2
Lightweight enough for modest shared hosting
cons: Smaller plugin and theme ecosystem than WordPress
Flat-file storage can become slower at very large post volumes
Admin interface feels dated compared to modern CMS platforms

faqs.question: Is FlatPress free?
faqs.answer: Yes, FlatPress is open-source software released under the GNU GPLv2 license.

faqs.question: Does FlatPress need a database?
faqs.answer: No, it stores all content as flat files instead of using a database.

faqs.question: Can I extend FlatPress with plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, it has a plugin and widget system for adding functionality.

faqs.question: How do I back up a FlatPress site?
faqs.answer: Since everything is stored as files, backing up is a matter of copying the site's files and folders.

use_cases.title: Simple personal blogging
use_cases.description: Run a blog without setting up or maintaining a database.
use_cases.audience: Independent bloggers on shared or low-resource hosting

use_cases.title: Portable, easy-to-migrate sites
use_cases.description: Move or back up a site by copying its file directory rather than exporting a database.
use_cases.audience: Site owners who want simple hosting migrations

enrichment.seo_meta_description: FlatPress is a free, open-source PHP blogging platform that stores posts as flat files instead of a database.

enrichment.short_description: Databaseless PHP blogging platform and flat-file CMS with fast content indexing, scheduled posts, and two-factor authentication.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HTMLy is an open-source, databaseless PHP blogging platform and flat-file CMS. It uses its own indexing approach to organize content by date, type, category, tag, or author, aiming to stay fast even on shared hosting with modest resources. The project is developed by Danpros and maintained on GitHub as danpros/htmly.

It includes archives, categories, tags, and full-text search, along with scheduled posts, custom fields, a sitemap and menu editor, and two-factor authentication for the admin login. Themes are downloaded and installed separately from the HTMLy site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: HTMLy is aimed at bloggers and site owners who want a fast, low-resource blogging platform without database administration, especially on shared or minimal hosting.

features.title: Flat-file, no-database architecture
features.description: Stores and indexes posts as files, avoiding database setup and maintenance entirely.

features.title: Custom fast indexing
features.description: Uses its own algorithm to keep content lookups by date, category, tag, or author performant as a site grows.

features.title: Scheduled posts and custom fields
features.description: Queue posts for future publication and attach custom metadata fields to content.

features.title: Two-factor authentication
features.description: Adds 2FA to the admin login for extra account security.

features.title: Menu editor and sitemap generation
features.description: Manage site navigation and generate a sitemap from the admin panel.

features.title: One-click updates
features.description: Update the platform from within the admin interface.

pros: No database to install or maintain
Free and open source
Built-in 2FA for admin login security
Lightweight enough for shared hosting with minimal PHP requirements
Full-text search and tag/category organization out of the box
cons: Requires several specific PHP extensions (Mbstring, XML, INTL, GD, ZIP)
Smaller theme and plugin ecosystem than WordPress
Themes must be downloaded and installed manually rather than through an in-app marketplace

faqs.question: Is HTMLy free?
faqs.answer: Yes, HTMLy is free and open source; its source code is available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does HTMLy require a database?
faqs.answer: No, it's a flat-file CMS that stores and indexes content as files.

faqs.question: What are HTMLy's server requirements?
faqs.answer: PHP 7.2 or later with the Mbstring, XML, INTL, GD, and ZIP extensions (ZIP is needed for backup functionality).

faqs.question: Does HTMLy support two-factor authentication?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes built-in 2FA for the admin login.

use_cases.title: Lightweight blogging on shared hosting
use_cases.description: Run a fast blog without needing database access or administration.
use_cases.audience: Bloggers on budget or shared hosting plans

use_cases.title: Large content archives
use_cases.description: Organize and search thousands of posts by date, category, tag, or author without a database backend.
use_cases.audience: Publishers with a large existing archive of posts

enrichment.seo_meta_description: HTMLy is a free, open-source, databaseless PHP blogging platform and flat-file CMS with search, tags, and 2FA.

enrichment.short_description: Minimalist Markdown blogging platform with no ads or tracking, offering a free tier and a low-cost premium plan for custom domains.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mataroa is a deliberately stripped-down blogging platform, described by its creator as a 'naked blogging platform, for minimalists.' Posts are written in Markdown and published on a mataroa.blog subdomain or a connected custom domain, with no ads, cookies, or tracking. Signup doesn't require an email address, and users can export all of their data at any time.

The platform has been running since 2020 and is currently maintained by a single developer. It's 100% open source under the AGPL license, with self-hosting and deployment documentation available on GitHub, and generates revenue through an optional $9/year premium plan, part of which funds carbon removal.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Mataroa is built for writers who want a distraction-free, minimal publishing tool and are put off by ad-heavy or feature-bloated blogging platforms. It also appeals to developers who want to self-host or fork a simple, open-source blogging codebase.

features.title: Markdown writing
features.description: Write posts in Markdown with support for code highlighting, tables, and footnotes.

features.title: Custom domains (premium)
features.description: Connect your own domain instead of using a mataroa.blog subdomain.

features.title: Post-by-email (premium)
features.description: Publish new posts by sending an email, without opening the web editor.

features.title: RSS and email newsletters
features.description: Readers can subscribe via RSS or receive new posts by email.

features.title: Full data export
features.description: Export all posts and data at any time for portability or self-hosting elsewhere.

features.title: API access
features.description: Interact with the platform programmatically through Mataroa's API.

features.title: No ads, cookies, or tracking
features.description: The platform runs without advertising, cookies, or analytics tracking on reader-facing pages.

pros: Genuinely minimal, distraction-free writing and reading experience
No ads, cookies, or tracking
No email required to sign up
Full data export and self-hosting option available
Low-cost premium plan at $9/year
cons: Maintained by a single developer, a noted sustainability risk
Free tier lacks custom domain and post-by-email support
Intentionally minimal features compared to full-featured blogging platforms

faqs.question: Is Mataroa free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is a free tier with core blogging features; a $9/year premium plan adds custom domains and post-by-email.

faqs.question: Do I need an email address to sign up?
faqs.answer: No, Mataroa does not require an email address to create an account.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Mataroa?
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform is 100% open source under the AGPL license with deployment documentation on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can I export my content?
faqs.answer: Yes, full data export is supported at any time.

use_cases.title: Minimalist personal blogging
use_cases.description: Publish Markdown posts without dealing with themes, plugins, or tracking scripts.
use_cases.audience: Writers who want a distraction-free publishing tool

use_cases.title: Self-hosted blog platform
use_cases.description: Fork or deploy the open-source codebase to run a custom instance.
use_cases.audience: Developers who want to self-host or build on Mataroa's codebase

features: Markdown blog on a mataroa.blog subdomain
Supports the platform and CO2 removal funding

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mataroa is a minimalist, ad-free Markdown blogging platform with a free tier and a $9/year premium plan for custom domains.

enrichment.short_description: French-origin, database-free CMS that stores content as XML files, with multi-user permissions and support for 11 languages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PluXml is a lightweight content management system that stores every page, post, and setting as XML files instead of relying on a database. Because there's no database, a full site can be copied, backed up, or moved between servers — even via a USB drive — by copying its files.

The platform supports static pages, categories, tags, article archives, comment management, and a media manager for images and documents, with five permission levels for multi-user setups. It's released under the GNU General Public License, requires PHP 7.2+ with the GD library, and runs on Apache, Nginx, or Lighttpd. PluXml ships with support for 11 languages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: PluXml is aimed at individuals, families, and small businesses who want a straightforward website or blog without managing a database, as well as multi-author sites that need simple, role-based permissions.

features.title: XML-based storage
features.description: Stores content as XML files rather than in a database, simplifying backup and portability.

features.title: Multi-user permission levels
features.description: Supports five distinct permission levels for managing multiple authors and editors.

features.title: Static pages, categories, and tags
features.description: Organize content with static pages, categories, tags, and chronological archives.

features.title: Media manager
features.description: Upload and manage images and documents through a built-in media library.

features.title: RSS feeds
features.description: Generates RSS feeds for articles, comments, and categories.

features.title: Responsive theming and plugins
features.description: Themes support mobile devices, and functionality can be extended through plugins.

features.title: URL rewriting
features.description: Supports clean, rewritten URLs for pages and posts.

pros: No database required, simplifying hosting and backups
Granular multi-user permissions with five access levels
Ships with support for 11 languages
Free and open source under the GPL
Lightweight PHP-only server requirements
cons: Smaller English-language community and documentation than mainstream CMS platforms
XML file storage may not scale as well as a database for very large sites
Smaller plugin and theme ecosystem than WordPress

faqs.question: Is PluXml free?
faqs.answer: Yes, PluXml is open-source software released under the GNU General Public License.

faqs.question: Does PluXml need a database?
faqs.answer: No, all content is stored as XML files instead of a database.

faqs.question: What are PluXml's server requirements?
faqs.answer: PHP 7.2 or later with the GD image library, running on Apache, Nginx, or Lighttpd.

faqs.question: Does PluXml support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, it ships with support for 11 languages, and originates from a French-speaking development community.

use_cases.title: Simple personal or family websites
use_cases.description: Build a straightforward website without database setup or technical maintenance.
use_cases.audience: Individuals, families, and hobbyists

use_cases.title: Multi-author small business sites
use_cases.description: Run a site with several contributors using role-based permission levels.
use_cases.audience: Small businesses and teams with multiple content editors

enrichment.seo_meta_description: PluXml is a free, open-source, database-free CMS that stores content as XML files and supports 11 languages.
